messages.content:
You are a technical writing assistant. You rephrase Python function docstrings while preserving their exact semantics.
Rules:
- Keep the function signature (name, parameters, return type annotation) exactly unchanged.
- Keep any import statements exactly unchanged.
- Keep doctest examples (lines starting with >>> or ...) exactly unchanged.
- Rephrase only the natural language description inside the docstring.
- Make each variant meaningfully different from the others.
- Return a JSON array of 4 strings, each containing one complete paraphrased function. No markdown fences, no other text.

def bf(planet1, planet2):
    '''
    There are eight planets in our solar system: the closerst to the Sun 
    is Mercury, the next one is Venus, then Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, 
    Uranus, Neptune.
    Write a function that takes two planet names as strings planet1 and planet2. 
    The function should return a tuple containing all planets whose orbits are 
    located between the orbit of planet1 and the orbit of planet2, sorted by 
    the proximity to the sun. 
    The function should return an empty tuple if planet1 or planet2
    are not correct planet names. 
    Examples
    bf("Jupiter", "Neptune") ==> ("Saturn", "Uranus")
    bf("Earth", "Mercury") ==> ("Venus")
    bf("Mercury", "Uranus") ==> ("Venus", "Earth", "Mars", "Jupiter", "Saturn")
    '''

samples.content:
[
"{\"def bf(planet1, planet2):\\n    '''\\n    The solar system contains eight planets listed in order from the Sun: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.\\nImplement a function that accepts two strings, planet1 and planet2, representing planet names.\\nThe function should yield a tuple of planets located between the orbits of the two given planets, arranged in order of increasing proximity to the Sun.\\nIn case either input planet name is invalid, return an empty tuple.\\nExamples\\nbf(\\\"Jupiter\\\", \\\"Neptune\\\") ==> (\\\"Saturn\\\", \\\"Uranus\\\")\\nbf(\\\"Earth\\\", \\\"Mercury\\\") ==> (\\\"Venus\\\")\\nbf(\\\"Mercury\\\", \\\"Uranus\\\") ==> (\\\"Venus\\\", \\\"Earth\\\", \\\"Mars\\\", \\\"Jupiter\\\", \\\"Saturn\\\")\\n    '''\"",
"{\"def bf(planet1, planet2):\\n    '''\\n    Our sun is orbited by eight planets: Mercury is closest, then Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and farthest is Neptune.\\nCreate a function that takes two string arguments, planet1 and planet2, which are names of planets.\\nIt must return a tuple of all planets that orbit between planet1 and planet2, sorted from the nearest to the Sun to the farthest.\\nAn empty tuple should be returned if either planet1 or planet2 is not a recognized planet name.\\nExamples\\nbf(\\\"Jupiter\\\", \\\"Neptune\\\") ==> (\\\"Saturn\\\", \\\"Uranus\\\")\\nbf(\\\"Earth\\\", \\\"Mercury\\\") ==> (\\\"Venus\\\")\\nbf(\\\"Mercury\\\", \\\"Uranus\\\") ==> (\\\"Venus\\\", \\\"Earth\\\", \\\"Mars\\\", \\\"Jupiter\\\", \\\"Saturn\\\")\\n    '''\"",
"{\"def bf(planet1, planet2):\\n    '''\\n    The planets of the solar system, in order from the Sun, are Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.\\nDevelop a function with parameters planet1 and planet2, both strings denoting planet names.\\nThis function should output a tuple comprising planets that have orbits between those of planet1 and planet2, ordered by their distance to the Sun.\\nIf either planet name provided is incorrect, the function returns an empty tuple.\\nExamples\\nbf(\\\"Jupiter\\\", \\\"Neptune\\\") ==> (\\\"Saturn\\\", \\\"Uranus\\\")\\nbf(\\\"Earth\\\", \\\"Mercury\\\") ==> (\\\"Venus\\\")\\nbf(\\\"Mercury\\\", \\\"Uranus\\\") ==> (\\\"Venus\\\", \\\"Earth\\\", \\\"Mars\\\", \\\"Jupiter\\\", \\\"Saturn\\\")\\n    '''\"",
"{\"def bf(planet1, planet2):\\n    '''\\n    Eight planets revolve around the Sun: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune, listed by increasing orbital distance.\\nWrite a function that accepts planet1 and planet2 as string inputs, representing planet names.\\nThe function must return a tuple containing all planets whose orbits lie between the orbits of planet1 and planet2, with the planets sorted by how close they are to the Sun.\\nAn empty tuple is to be returned when either planet1 or planet2 is not a valid planet name.\\nExamples\\nbf(\\\"Jupiter\\\", \\\"Neptune\\\") ==> (\\\"Saturn\\\", \\\"Uranus\\\")\\nbf(\\\"Earth\\\", \\\"Mercury\\\") ==> (\\\"Venus\\\")\\nbf(\\\"Mercury\\\", \\\"Uranus\\\") ==> (\\\"Venus\\\", \\\"Earth\\\", \\\"Mars\\\", \\\"Jupiter\\\", \\\"Saturn\\\")\\n    '''\""
]
